channel.title: Dungeons & Dragons on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Dungeons & Dragons on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Discovering D&D In 1979
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about discovering DnD in 1979.

Dungeons and Dragons was like nothing else that had ever been out before And I want to try to explain it to you. Because I was trying to explain it to a, coworker. Because if, you started playing DnD on a computer where there’s no DM, the computer handles it all. There are another players, There’s a tutorial that walks you through the basics so you have an idea of what to expect. You don’t have to learn the rules. I mean, you want to learn how things work, but you don’t have to learn how to run the rules.

item.title: AD&D Design Pillars
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Advanced Dungeons and Dragons design pillars.

I’ve talked about design pillars on this channel. I have a whole video on it. I’ll link it below.

In short, these pillars are like the bedrock, the foundation of your game, from which all features and all content spring from and to which they serve. It’s basically you saying: “This is essentially what my game is about, and everything I’m going to talk about — from a mechanic to the storyline, to elements of the setting — will make sense with respect to those pillars. I want to talk about what AD and D’s design pillars were, why it was my first tabletop role playing game. It was the very first thing that told me what a role playing game could be, which, of course, very much influenced my career, but its design pillars were never clearly stated anywhere. The game itself sprang from strategy tabletop games, where they were essentially battle simulations and occasionally a hero character would like emerge from that. You’d have a bunch of battles and there was one surviving lieutenant or captain that survived battle after battle after battle and you kind, you were kind of starting to get attached to them and a uh, Dungeons and Dragons, and then later AD and D, which formalized a lot of uh, D and D’s rules, were people wanting to play characters from those sessions and have them become heroes where they would, you know, go into dungeons and fight villains and gain all kinds of loot and maybe get their castle and become a hero From all this. What I always took from Dn D as its fundamental pillar was, with reference to fantasy, because that’s what it was aimed at — is that a any character you could think of, whether you liked Lord of the Rings or whether you like the Hiboreian age, if you wanted to play Aragorn or you wanted.

item.title: AD&D Questions From Teenage Tim
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you some — well, read you some advanced Dungeons and Dragons questions from teenage Tim.

What, what is this? Are you? », what? Last week’s fun Friday I had a list of questions that me and the team assembled that we asked Watsi about the Temple of Elemental Evil module that we’re making into a computer game. That made me wonder, because I know I had done the same thing. I think it was 1980, so I would have been 14,, maybe 15. And I had a set of questions that I sent to TSR, because when I was young I was super fearless about just sending mail to companies. I have a video on how to get a job where I show you the designs that I sent to Atari when I was 13,, I think.

item.title: Questions To WotC About ToEE
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share with you questions I sent to Wizards of the Coast about Temple of Elemental Evil.

This is a fun Friday, so this is not super serious, but it is important, I think, to dive into. Uh, let me tell you why I’m doing this. I’ve been looking for the test plan that I made for Tempo of Elemental Evil that I sent to Atari QA. I was planning to do a video on that. I’m still planning to do a video on that, but I can’t find the test plan And I know I wrote one.

item.title: D&D Influences (By Edition)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about DND influences by addition.

Mostly this is influences on me and my work, but in one case it’s going to be my work influencing back into D and D, which I think is really cool. Anyway, this is response to Y l333 who says: hey, Tim, I don’t know about others, but I would love to hear about which editions of D and you’ve played, especially in the TSR days, what D and D books you own and which additions are your favorite and have had the most influence on your games. I’ve done a lot of talking about D and D here and there and I’ve done D and D stories and all that I will talk about, and I’ve talked about influences, tabletop influences, of non D andd stuff. So let’s just talk about Dungeons and Dragons. I have mentioned several times that the way I got introduced to Dungeons and Dragons was a little unusual.

item.title: My AD&D Mods
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about modding the original advanced Dungeons and Dragons.

But before I go into that, let me just say that a lot of people ask me about modding and what I think of current mods and mods for different games. I love them. I’ve said I loved them.

I’m not going to review them, but I’ve been playing and enjoying mods since probably back in the 90s with quake and as recently as Fallout 4. The last time I played it I did a playthrough and then, after I finished the main story arc, I loaded it a bunch of mods just to see what people were doing with that game. And also, when I tell people, if you’re thinking about getting a job in the game industry and you should make a demo, modding an existing game is perfectly okay. If you have an idea for a cool new feature and you can take an existing game and just mod that feature in just to show off how it. Work and how it makes the game and you can demonstrate, like, how it makes the game better, perfect. We hired someone for The Outer Worlds based on a mod he made for Skyrim. So it happens people pay attention to that stuff and I can tell you that that guy’s mod, you know, moved his resume to the top of the pile. So if you’re thinking about modding, do it.

item.title: My Brother's D&D Miniatures
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk to you about my brother’s DND D Miniatures.

Let me give you some background. I started playing D and D in the late 7s and it was a bit of a learning curve to play D and D. It was nothing like me or any of my friends had ever played before and we got the books and at first we. The first book out was the monster manual, so I had a basic set and a monster manual and the monster manual was for ad and then there was the basic set for basic DND and I kind of learned how to play from that. Then I got a players handbook which contradicted so much stuff in the basic D and manual.

item.title: Appendix N
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about appendix, and Josh James 582, asked for my thoughts on appendix n, which is in the DM’s guide for advanced Dungeons and Dragons, which lets me show off something I’m super excited about.

I got Gary gygax to sign this for me at a convention in Los Angeles in 92. And later, when I made Temple volento evil, I asked him if he remembered. He was sweet and said he did. I don’t think he really did, but he then said you mentioned Bard’s Tale Construction Set, and I was like maybe he did remember.

item.title: Picking Your Best Class in AD&D
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about picking your best class in a, d and d.

Why? Because I found This is something I sent to Dragon publishing. Oh, it’s not gonna work. Um in 1980, Something the postmark has been worn off for a submission to be published in the dragon magazine, although I think they dropped the by then used to be the dragon and then it just became dragon. But I submitted this to Roger Moore who was the editor, saying: hey, you wrote something like this, but since then Unearthed arcan has come out.
